name: Mock Test - 03
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: There is a uniform field of strength {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{V}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} along the y-axis. A body of mass 1g and charge {tex}{\rm{1}}0{ ^{ - {\rm{6}}}}{/tex} C is projected into the field from the origin along the positive x-axis with a velocity of {tex}{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ m}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} . Its speed (in {tex}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} ) after 10 second will be (neglect gravitation)
questions.question_html: There is a uniform field of strength {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{3}}}{\rm{V}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} along the y-axis. A body of mass 1g and charge {tex}{\rm{1}}0{ ^{ - {\rm{6}}}}{/tex}C is projected into the field from the origin along the positive x-axis with a velocity of {tex}{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ m}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex}. Its speed (in {tex}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex}) after 10 second will be (neglect gravitation)
questions.explanation: Given v x = 10 m/s Since the electric field is in y-direction, so the acceleration is in y-direction. {tex}{a_{y = }}\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right) = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 6}} \times {{10}^3}}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 1\;m/s^2{/tex} So, velocity in y direction v y = a y t = 1 {tex}\times 10 = 10\;m/s{/tex} So, {tex}v = \sqrt {{v_x}^2 + {v_y}^2} = \sqrt {{{10}^2} + {{10}^2}} = 10\sqrt 2 \;m/s{/tex}

questions.question_text: Electric potential of earth is taken to be zero, because earth is a good
questions.question_html: Electric potential of earth is taken to be zero, because earth is a good
questions.explanation: Earth is a conducting sphere of large capacitance. {tex}V=\frac{q}{C}{/tex} As C is very large, so V {tex}\times{/tex} 0 for all finite charges.

questions.question_text: The wire of the potentiometer has resistance 4 ohms and length 1 m. It is connected to a cell of e.m.f. 2 volts and internal resistance 1 ohm, if a cell of e.m.f. 1.2 volt is balanced by it, the balancing length will be
questions.question_html: The wire of the potentiometer has resistance 4 ohms and length 1 m. It is connected to a cell of e.m.f. 2 volts and internal resistance 1 ohm, if a cell of e.m.f. 1.2 volt is balanced by it, the balancing length will be
questions.explanation: If the battery has e.m.f E, resistance of the potentiometer is R and the internal resistance of the battery is r, then the current I flowing in the potentiometer wire is given by, {tex}I = \frac{E}{{\left( {R + r} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}I = \frac{2}{{\left( {4 + 1} \right)}}{/tex} I = 0.4 A The potential difference V across the potentiometer {tex}V = I \times R{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = 0.4 \times 4{/tex} V = 1.6V The potential gradient = ( potential drop across the potentiometer)/ length of the potentiometer wire) {tex} = \frac{V}{l}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.6}}{1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} Potential gradient = 1.6V/m The emf of the cell {tex}{E_1}=\left( {Potential\;gradient \times Balancing\;length} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow L = \frac{{{E_1}}}{{Potential\;gradient}} = \frac{{1.2}}{{1.6}}{/tex} L = 0.75 m or L = 75 cm

questions.question_text: The unit of charge is
questions.question_html: The unit of charge is
questions.explanation: The SI unit of quantity of electric charge is the coulomb, which is equivalent to about 6.242×10 18 e (e is the charge of a proton).

questions.question_text: If the potential of a capacitor having capacity 8 pF is increased from 10 V to 20 V, then increase in its energy will be:
questions.question_html: If the potential of a capacitor having capacity 8 pF is increased from 10 V to 20 V, then increase in its energy will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}\Delta U=U_{2}-U_{1}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-6}\left(20^{2}-10^{2}\right){/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 J = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 J

questions.question_text: The internal resistance of a 2.1 V cell which gives a current of 0.2 A through a resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is:
questions.question_html: The internal resistance of a 2.1 V cell which gives a current of 0.2 A through a resistance of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is:

questions.question_text: In an inductor of self-inductance L = 2mH, current changes with time according to relation I = t 2 e -t . At what time, emf is zero?
questions.question_html: In an inductor of self-inductance L = 2mH, current changes with time according to relation I = t2e-t. At what time, emf is zero?
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=-L \frac{d I}{d t}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left(t^{2} e^{-t}\right)=0{/tex} or 2t {tex}\times{/tex} t 2 e -t (-1) = 0 or te -t ((2 - t) = 0 or t = 2s {tex}\left[t e^{-t} \neq 0\right]{/tex}

questions.question_text: The magnetic susceptibility is negative for
questions.question_html: The magnetic susceptibility is negative for
questions.explanation: The susceptibility of a diamagnetic material is small and negative because it develops a weak magnetization in the opposite direction of the magnetizing field.

questions.question_text: The self-inductance of a coil having 500 turns is 50 mH. The magnetic flux through the cross-sectional area of the coil, while the current through it is 8 mA, is found to be:
questions.question_html: The self-inductance of a coil having 500 turns is 50 mH. The magnetic flux through the cross-sectional area of the coil, while the current through it is 8 mA, is found to be:
questions.explanation: {tex}\phi=L I=50 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~Wb}{/tex} {tex}=4 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} Wb

questions.question_text: The current in a series LCR circuit excited by AC of frequency {tex}\omega {/tex} in general is
questions.question_html: The current in a series LCR circuit excited by AC of frequency {tex}\omega {/tex} in general is
questions.explanation: I = I m sin( {tex}\omega {\rm{t }} + {\rm{ }}\phi {/tex} ) The above expression is for instantaneous value of current in an AC Circuit and since in an LCR circuit current is same across the circuit, the above expression holds valid for the entire LCR series circuit.

questions.question_text: A proton and an alpha particle enter in a uniform magnetic field with the same velocity. The time period of rotation of the alpha particle will be:
questions.question_html: A proton and an alpha particle enter in a uniform magnetic field with the same velocity. The time period of rotation of the alpha particle will be:

options.text: same as that of the proton
options.html: same as that of the proton

options.text: four times that of the proton
options.html: four times that of the proton

options.text: two times that of the proton
options.html: two times that of the proton

options.text: three times that of the proton
options.html: three times that of the proton

questions.correct_option_text: two times that of the proton
questions.explanation: As {tex}T=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ T_{p}=\frac{2 \pi m_{p}}{e B}{/tex} {tex}T_{\alpha}=\frac{2 \pi \times 4 m_{p}}{2 e B}=2 T_{p}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A potentiometer circuit has been set up for finding the internal resistance of a given cell. The main battery, used across the potentiometer wire, has an emf of 2.0 V and a negligible internal resistance. The potentiometer wire itself is 4 m long. When the resistance R, connected across the given cell, has values of infinity 9.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} the balancing lengths , on the potentiometer wire are found to be 3 and 2.85 m, respectively. The value of internal resistance of the cell is:
questions.question_html:
A potentiometer circuit has been set up for finding the internal resistance of a given cell. The main battery, used across the potentiometer wire, has an emf of 2.0 V and a negligible internal resistance. The potentiometer wire itself is 4 m long. When the resistance R, connected across the given cell, has values of

  1. infinity
  2. 9.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

the balancing lengths, on the potentiometer wire are found to be 3 and 2.85 m, respectively. The value of internal resistance of the cell is:
questions.explanation: r = {tex}\left(\frac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}-1\right) R=\left(\frac{3}{2.85}-1\right) \times 9.5{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.15}{2.85} \times 9.5=0.5 \Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: Two inductors each of inductance L are joined in parallel. What is their equivalent inductance?
questions.question_html: Two inductors each of inductance L are joined in parallel. What is their equivalent inductance?
questions.explanation: If M is negligible, then for parallel combination of two inductors, {tex}L_{e q}=\frac{L_{1} L_{2}}{L_{1}+L_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{L \times L}{L+L}=\frac{L}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Alternating current is so called because
questions.question_html: Alternating current is so called because

options.text: Voltage alternates between 10 V and 15 V
options.html: Voltage alternates between 10 V and 15 V

options.text: Current is switched on and off alternately
options.html: Current is switched on and off alternately

options.text: Voltage alternates between 0 V and 5 V
options.html: Voltage alternates between 0 V and 5 V

options.text: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time
options.html: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time

questions.correct_option_text: Polarity alternates from + to – and – to + with time
questions.explanation: Alternating Current is the current in which the polarity of source continuously changes on a fixed frequency. So the positive and the negative terminals ‘alternate’.

questions.question_text: A charge q {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed at the centre of a cube of side 0.1 m. Then the electric flux diverging from each face of this cube is
questions.question_html: A charge q {tex}\mu{/tex}C is placed at the centre of a cube of side 0.1 m. Then the electric flux diverging from each face of this cube is
questions.explanation: {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{\text { Net charge enclosed }}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q \times 10^{-6}}{6 \varepsilon_{0}} {/tex}

questions.question_text: In a system of n point charges electric potential at a point P having a distance {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} from the charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: In a system of n point charges electric potential at a point P having a distance {tex}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} from the charge {tex}{{\rm{q}}_{\rm{i}}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Electric potential at a point V is the work done in bringing unit positive charge from infinity to the point in an electric field. When there are multiple charges, the net potential will be the scaler sum of the potentials due to the individual charges because electric potential is a scaler quantity thus {tex}{V_1} = \frac{{{q_1}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_1}}};{V_2} = \frac{{{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_2}}};...{V_i} = \frac{{{q_i}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_i}}}{/tex} And V = V 1 + V 2 + .... + V i {tex}= \sum\limits_1^i {\frac{{{q_i}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r_i}}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\sum\limits_1^i {\frac{{{q_i}}}{{{r_i}}}} {/tex}

questions.question_text: Magnetic moment of a single-turn current loop of aluminium wire of area A, carrying a current I in a wire of diameter d is
questions.question_html: Magnetic moment of a single-turn current loop of aluminium wire of area A, carrying a current I in a wire of diameter d is
questions.explanation: The magnetic moment, m = NIA Here N = 1, Thus, m = IA

questions.question_text: If the rotational velocity of dynamo armature is doubled, then induced emf will become:
questions.question_html: If the rotational velocity of dynamo armature is doubled, then induced emf will become:
questions.explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} i.e., {tex}\varepsilon \propto \omega{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{2 \omega}{\omega}=2{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of 1 Bohr magneton is: [Given h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C and m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg]
questions.question_html: The value of 1 Bohr magneton is: [Given h = 6.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C and me = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg]
questions.explanation: 1 Bohr magneton = {tex}\frac{eh}{4\pi m_e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.6 \times {10^{-19}} \times 6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{4\pi \times 9.1 \times 10 ^{-31}}{/tex} = 9.27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 Am 2

questions.question_text: The voltage of clouds is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 V with respect to ground. In a lightning strike lasting 0.1 s, a charge of 4 C is delivered to the ground. The power of the lightning strike is:
questions.question_html: The voltage of clouds is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 V with respect to ground. In a lightning strike lasting 0.1 s, a charge of 4 C is delivered to the ground. The power of the lightning strike is:
questions.explanation: {tex}P=\frac{W}{t}=\frac{q V}{t}=\frac{4 \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{0.1} W{/tex} = 160 MW

questions.question_text: Five charges q 1 , q 2 , q 3 , q 4 , and q 5 are fixed at their positions as shown in Fig., S is a Gaussian surface. The Gauss’s law is given by {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{s}} \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_html:
Five charges q1 , q2 , q3 , q4, and q5 are fixed at their positions as shown in Fig., S is a Gaussian surface. The Gauss’s law is given by {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{s}} \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Both E on the LHS and q on the RHS will have contributions from q 2 and q 4 only.
options.html: Both E on the LHS and q on the RHS will have contributions from q2 and q4 only.

options.text: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q 1 , q 3 , and q 5 only.
options.html: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q1, q3, and q5 only.

options.text: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from q 1 , q 5 , and q 3 while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q 2 and q 4 only.
options.html: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from q1, q5, and q3 while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q2 and q4 only.

options.text: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q 2 and q 4 only.
options.html: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q2 and q4 only.

questions.correct_option_text: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q 2 and q 4 only.
questions.explanation: According to the Gauss' theorem, the term q on the right side of the equation includes the sum of all charges enclosed by the surface (irrespective of the position of the charges inside the surface). If the surface is so chosen that there are some charges inside and some outside, the electric field on the left side of the equation will be due to all charges (both inside as well as outside) Hence, E on LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from charges q 2 and q 4 only. Hence option (a) is correct.

questions.question_text: In an a.c. circuit, the potential differences across an inductance and resistance joined in series are respectively 16 V and 20 V. The total potential difference of the source is
questions.question_html: In an a.c. circuit, the potential differences across an inductance and resistance joined in series are respectively 16 V and 20 V. The total potential difference of the source is
questions.explanation: For an RL-circuit, {tex}V_{\text {eff }}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+V_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{20^{2}+16^{2}}=\sqrt{656}=25.6 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Magnetic flux (Φ B ) through a surface S is given by
questions.question_html: Magnetic flux (ΦB) through a surface S is given by
questions.explanation: if dS is area of an element of any arbitrary shape and magnetic field at this element is B. The total magnetic flux through the surface is the sum of the contributions from the individual area elements.

questions.question_text: Due to earth's magnetic field, the charged cosmic rays particles:
questions.question_html: Due to earth's magnetic field, the charged cosmic rays particles:

options.text: require less kinetic energy to reach the equator than pole
options.html: require less kinetic energy to reach the equator than pole

options.text: require greater kinetic energy to reach the equator than pole
options.html: require greater kinetic energy to reach the equator than pole

questions.correct_option_text: require greater kinetic energy to reach the equator than pole
questions.explanation: At the equator, the earth's magnetic field is perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charged cosmic ray particles. As these particles get deflected due to the earth's magnetic field, so they require greater kinetic energy to reach the equator than the poles.

questions.question_text: An alternating electric field, of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} , is applied across the dees (radius = R) of a cyclotron that is being used to accelerate protons (mass = m). The operating magnetic field (B) used in the cyclotron and the kinetic energy (K) of the proton beam, produced by it, are given by
questions.question_html: An alternating electric field, of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex}, is applied across the dees (radius = R) of a cyclotron that is being used to accelerate protons (mass = m). The operating magnetic field (B) used in the cyclotron and the kinetic energy (K) of the proton beam, produced by it, are given by

questions.question_text: The electric current in a circular coil of two turns produces a magnetic induction of 0.2 T at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of four turns. The magnetic induction at the centre of the coil now is, in tesla
questions.question_html: The electric current in a circular coil of two turns produces a magnetic induction of 0.2 T at its centre. The coil is unwound and is rewound into a circular coil of four turns. The magnetic induction at the centre of the coil now is, in tesla
questions.explanation: We know that, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 r}{/tex} If the number of turns is doubled the radius is halved. Therefore, {tex}B^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_{0}(2 N) I}{2(r / 2)}=4 B=4 \times 0.2=0.8 \mathrm{T}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The electric field between the plates of a fully charged capacitor is E. If a material of dielectric constant K is introduced between the plates, the electric field at a point between the plates
questions.question_html: The electric field between the plates of a fully charged capacitor is E. If a material of dielectric constant K is introduced between the plates, the electric field at a point between the plates
questions.explanation: Charge on the plates of the capacitor is q = CV When a dielectric slab (dielectric constant, K) introduced between the plates of the capacitor, capacity of the capacitor becomes K times that of it had in case of air. Thus, C (dielectric) = KC Therefore, if dielectric slab is introduced then charge on the plates remain the same. But potential difference V° between the plates decreases as q = C (dielectric) V° or V° = {tex}\frac{q} {C_{(dielectric)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{{KC}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{K}{/tex} Since K is greater than 1, therefore, V° < V and electric field E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} Here d is the distance between the plates of the capacitor. Therefore E°, electric field between the plates in presence of dielectric slab, E° = {tex}\frac{V ^\circ}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{Kd}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{E}{K}{/tex} That is E° < E.

questions.question_text: A metallic solid sphere is placed in a uniform electric field. In the figure, which path will the lines of force follow?
questions.question_html: A metallic solid sphere is placed in a uniform electric field. In the figure, which path will the lines of force follow?
questions.explanation: The electric field is always normal to the surface of a conductor. On the surface of a metallic solid sphere, the electrical field is oriented normally (i.e. directed towards the centre of the sphere) and inside the conductor electric field is zero. So, 4th line is correct.

questions.question_text: Alternating current can not be measured by d.c. ammeter, because:
questions.question_html: Alternating current can not be measured by d.c. ammeter, because:

options.text: average value of current of complete cycle is zero
options.html: average value of current of complete cycle is zero

options.text: a.c. cannot pass through a.c. ammeter
options.html: a.c. cannot pass through a.c. ammeter

questions.correct_option_text: average value of current of complete cycle is zero
questions.explanation: The average value of alternating current over a complete cycle is zero

questions.question_text: A uniformly wound long solenoid of inductance L and resistance R is broken into two equal parts in the ratio {tex}\frac {\eta}1{/tex} , which are then joined in parallel. This combination is then joined to a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} . The time constant of the circuit is
questions.question_html: A uniformly wound long solenoid of inductance L and resistance R is broken into two equal parts in the ratio {tex}\frac {\eta}1{/tex}, which are then joined in parallel. This combination is then joined to a cell of emf {tex}\varepsilon {/tex}. The time constant of the circuit is
questions.explanation: {tex}L_{1}=\left(\frac{\eta}{\eta+1}\right) L, R_{1}=\left(\frac{\eta}{\eta+1}\right) R{/tex} {tex}L_{2}=\left(\frac{1}{\eta+1}\right) L, R_{2}=\left(\frac{1}{\eta+1}\right) R{/tex} {tex}L_{n e t}=\frac{L_{1} L_{2}}{L_{1}+L_{2}}=\frac{\eta L L}{(\eta+1)(\eta L+L)}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}R_{n e t}=\frac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{\eta R R}{(\eta+1)(\eta R+R)}{/tex} {tex}\tau_L=\frac{L_{n e t}}{R_{n e t}}=\frac{L}{R}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Tesla is the unit of
questions.question_html: Tesla is the unit of
questions.explanation: magnetic induction

questions.question_text: Given magnetic field B, area A and length l of a solenoid. The magnetic energy per unit volume is
questions.question_html: Given magnetic field B, area A and length l of a solenoid. The magnetic energy per unit volume is

questions.question_text: The potential difference (V A - V B ) between the points A and B in the given figure is:
questions.question_html: The potential difference (VA - VB) between the points A and B in the given figure is:
questions.explanation: V B = V A - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 3 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = V A - 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} V A - V B = 9 V Alternative V A - V B = 9 - 0 = 9 V

questions.question_text: A parallel combination of 0.1 M {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistor and a 10 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor is connected across a 1.5 V source of negligible resistance. The time (in seconds) required for the capacitor to get charged up to 0.75 V is approximately
questions.question_html: A parallel combination of 0.1 M{tex}\Omega {/tex} resistor and a 10 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor is connected across a 1.5 V source of negligible resistance. The time (in seconds) required for the capacitor to get charged up to 0.75 V is approximately
questions.explanation: There is no resistance in the part of the circuit containing battery and the capacitor, i.e., R = 0 in this circuit. Hence the time constant which is equal to RC would be equal to zero.

questions.question_text: Which of the following characteristics of electrons determines the current in a conductor?
questions.question_html: Which of the following characteristics of electrons determines the current in a conductor?

options.text: Both drift velocity and thermal velocity.
options.html: Both drift velocity and thermal velocity.

questions.explanation: As I = Anev d ’ So current I {tex}\propto{/tex} v d Although I also depend on n, the number of free electrons which increases on increasing temperature which makes more collision between electrons increases the resistance or decrease the current and we obtain a large amount of current even drift velocity is small because electron no. Density is very large.

questions.question_text: To reduce the reasonant frequency in an LCR series circuit with a generator
questions.question_html: To reduce the reasonant frequency in an LCR series circuit with a generator

options.text: dielectric in the capacitor should be removed
options.html: dielectric in the capacitor should be removed

options.text: the iron core of the inductor should be removed
options.html: the iron core of the inductor should be removed

options.text: another capacitor should be added in parallel to the first
options.html: another capacitor should be added in parallel to the first

options.text: the generator frequency should be reduced
options.html: the generator frequency should be reduced

questions.correct_option_text: another capacitor should be added in parallel to the first
questions.explanation: Resonant frequency in an L-C-R circuit is given by {tex}v_{0}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}{/tex} , {tex}v_{0} {/tex} is the frequency and L is the inductor and C for the capacitor. If L or C increases, the resonant frequency will reduce. To increase capacitance, we must connect another capacitor parallel to the first.

questions.question_text: Kamla peddles a stationary bicycle the pedals of the bicycle are attached to a 100 turn coil of area {tex}0.{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ }}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} . The coil rotates at half a revolution per second and it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.02 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the coil. What is the maximum voltage generated in the coil?
questions.question_html: Kamla peddles a stationary bicycle the pedals of the bicycle are attached to a 100 turn coil of area {tex}0.{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ }}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}. The coil rotates at half a revolution per second and it is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.02 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the coil. What is the maximum voltage generated in the coil?
questions.explanation: Maximum voltage is given by: {tex}{e_0} = NBA\omega {/tex} but, {tex}\omega = 2\pi n = 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.5{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{e_0} = NBA\omega = 100 \times 0.02 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.5 = 0.628{\text{V}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A copper rod is suspended in a nonhomogeneous magnetic field region. The rod when in equilibrium will align itself
questions.question_html: A copper rod is suspended in a nonhomogeneous magnetic field region. The rod when in equilibrium will align itself

options.text: in the direction in which it was originally suspended
options.html: in the direction in which it was originally suspended

options.text: in the region where the magnetic field is weakest and parallel to the direction of the magnetic field there
options.html: in the region where the magnetic field is weakest and parallel to the direction of the magnetic field there

options.text: in the region where the magnetic field is weakest and perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field there
options.html: in the region where the magnetic field is weakest and perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field there

options.text: in the region where the magnetic field is strongest
options.html: in the region where the magnetic field is strongest

questions.correct_option_text: in the region where the magnetic field is weakest and perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field there
questions.explanation: Copper is diamagnetic. When suspended in a non-uniform magnetic field, its rod will align itself in the region where the field is weakest and perpendicular to the direction of the field so that repulsion is minimum.

questions.question_text: The output of a step-down transformer is measured to be 24 V when connected to a 12 watt light bulb. The value of the peak current is
questions.question_html: The output of a step-down transformer is measured to be 24 V when connected to a 12 watt light bulb. The value of the peak current is
questions.explanation: According to the problem output/secondary voltage V s = 24V Power associated with secondary P s = 12W {tex}I_{S}=\frac{P_{S}}{V_{S}}=\frac{12}{24}=0.5 \mathrm{A}{/tex} The amplitude of the current in the secondary winding {tex}I_{0}=I_{S} \sqrt{2}{/tex} = (0.5) (1.414) = 0.707 {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{A}{/tex} thus the value of peak current is given by = 0.707A = {tex}1\over\sqrt 2{/tex} A

questions.question_text: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced for four resistors R 1 , R 2 , R 3 and R 4 with a cell of emf 1.46 V. The cell is now replaced by another cell of emf 1.08 V. To obtain the balance again

questions.correct_option_text: No resistance needs to be changed
questions.explanation: The balance point of the Wheatstone’s bridge is determined by the ratio of the resistances. The change in the emf of the external battery will have no effect on the balance point.

questions.question_text: In producing chlorine by electrolysis 100 kW power at 125 V is being consumed. How much chlorine per minute is liberated? (E.C.E. of chlorine is 0.367 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 kg)
questions.question_html: In producing chlorine by electrolysis 100 kW power at 125 V is being consumed. How much chlorine per minute is liberated? (E.C.E. of chlorine is 0.367 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 kg)
questions.explanation: m = Zlt = {tex}Z \frac{P}{V} t{/tex} = 0.367 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times \frac{100 \ \times \ 10^{3}}{125} \times{/tex} 60 kg = 17.61 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 kg

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way. Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other. 4 cells each of emf 2 V and internal resistance of 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel to a load resistor of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Then the current through the load resistor is
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way.
Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other.


4 cells each of emf 2 V and internal resistance of 1{tex}\Omega{/tex} are connected in parallel to a load resistor of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Then the current through the load resistor is
questions.explanation: I = {tex}\frac{m E}{m R+r}{/tex} ,m = number of cells = 4 E = 2V, R = 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , r = 1 {tex}\Omega{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{8}{8+1}=\frac{8}{9}{/tex} = 0.888 A

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way. Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other. Two identical non-ideal batteries are connected in parallel. Consider the following statements. The equivalent emf is smaller than either of the two emfs. The equivalent internal resistance is smaller than either of the two internal resistances.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way.
Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other.


Two identical non-ideal batteries are connected in parallel. Consider the following statements.

  1. The equivalent emf is smaller than either of the two emfs.
  2. The equivalent internal resistance is smaller than either of the two internal resistances.
questions.explanation: Let two cells of emfs {tex}\varepsilon_1{/tex} and {tex}\varepsilon_2{/tex} and of internal resistance r 1 and r 2 respectively are connected in parallel. The equivalent emf is given by {tex}\varepsilon_{e q}=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} p_{2}+\varepsilon_{2} \gamma}{n+r_{2}}{/tex} ... (i) The equivalent internal resistance is given by {tex}\frac{1}{r_{e q}}=\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}r_{c q}=\frac{\gamma_{1} r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}}{/tex} ... (ii) Let us consider, two cells connected in parallel of same emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} and same internal resistance r. From equation (i), we get {tex}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\varepsilon r+\varepsilon r}{r+r}=\varepsilon{/tex} From equation (ii), we get {tex}r_{\text {eq }}=\frac{r^{2}}{r+r}=\frac{r}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way. Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other. To draw the maximum current from a combination of cells, how should the cells be grouped?
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

A single cell provides a feeble current. In order to get a higher current in a circuit, we often use a combination of cells. A combination of cells is called a battery. Cells can be joined in series, parallel or in a mixed way.
Two cells are said to be connected in series when negative terminal of one cell is connected to positive terminal of the other cell and so on. Two cells are said to be connected in parallel if positive terminal of each cell is connected to one point and negative terminal of each cell connected to the other point. In mixed grouping of cells, a certain number of identical cells are joined in series, and all such rows are then connected in parallel with each other.


To draw the maximum current from a combination of cells, how should the cells be grouped?
